title: Dexcom warns patients as stolen glucose sensors hit the market
summary: Medical device maker Dexcom said on Tuesday some of its glucose sensors ‚Äãslated for destruction were instead stolen and ‚Äåsold, prompting the company to warn users not to use products from two affected lots.

May 26 (Reuters) - Medical device maker Dexcom

(DXCM.O), opens new tab

said on Tuesday some of its glucose sensors ‚Äãslated for destruction were instead stolen and ‚Äåsold, prompting the company to warn users not to use products from two affected lots.

  • Dexcom ‚Äãsaid the affected G7 sensors, from ‚Äãlot numbers 1725204004 and 1725069002, had been ‚ÅÝmarked as scrap as they did ‚Äãnot meet company standards.

  • The sensors were stolen ‚Äãduring a destruction process and later sold by third parties, with Dexcom tracing sales to Pharmsource LLC, ‚Äãwhich is not an authorized distributor.

  • One ‚Äãof the affected lots may carry an increased risk ‚Äåof ‚ÅÝskin infection, while the other lot has a higher chance of producing no readings at all, DexCom said.

  • The issue mainly affects ‚Äãusers in ‚Äãthe United ‚ÅÝStates, and Dexcom said it is notifying customers and offering ‚Äãreplacements.

  • Dexcom said there have been no ‚Äãreported ‚ÅÝsevere adverse events linked to the stolen sensors.

  • The company said it is working with ‚ÅÝthe ‚ÄãU.S. Food and Drug ‚ÄãAdministration and other authorities to investigate the theft.

Reporting by Kunal ‚ÄãDas in Bengaluru; Editing by Vijay Kishore
